data.title: Comstock Resources: Production Declines Despite $1 Billion In Capex
data.description:
Comstock Resources (NYSE:CRK) lowered its production guidance for 2025 by approximately 6% while keeping its capex budget unchanged. This results in a projection that it could end up with close to $150 million in full-year cash burn now.

Comstock still is expected to have plenty of liquidity, but I've reduced its estimated value by $1 to approximately $13.50 per share now in a long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment.

This is due to Comstock's weaker production results and the impact of lower 2025 and 2026 natural gas strip prices on its projected near-term free cash flow.

I thought Comstock was very overvalued before, but I believe it is only moderately overvalued (for a long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario) now that its shares are down close to 50% from their highs.

Q2 2025 Results

Comstock averaged 1,233 MMCFE per day in Q2 2025 production, which was towards the low-end of its guidance for 1,225 to 1,325 MMCFE per day in average production during the quarter. Comstock's capex was within expectations for the quarter, while its per unit operating costs were near the low-end of its guidance.

Weaker realized prices (as well as slightly higher capex) during Q2 2025 (compared to Q1 2025) contributed to a $79 million free cash deficit from operations during the quarter. This was around zero for Q1 2025.

Guidance Revision

Comstock made a noticeable negative revision to its guidance for 2025. It now expects 1,225 to 1,325 MMCFE per day in average production during the year, which (at guidance midpoint) is a 6% reduction from its prior guidance. Comstock's D&C capex budget was unchanged, while it added $10 million to its acreage budget for the year.

Thus we may have a situation (at guidance midpoint) where Comstock sees a 12% year-over-year decline in production despite spending around $1 billion on D&C capex.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025E
Production (MMCFE per Day) 1,373 1,438 1,442 1,275
D&C Capex ($ Million) $1,032 $1,272 $902 $1,050

Comstock continues to de-risk its Western Haynesville acreage, but has added back a legacy Haynesville rig in an attempt to reverse some of those production declines.

While it has made significant progress with de-risking the Western Haynesville and becoming more capital efficient there, its legacy Haynesville acreage still appears to offer better returns at this stage. As well, there have been some midstream issues as it builds out its Western Haynesville infrastructure, and that may slightly delay some wells being turned online.

2H 2025 Outlook

Comstock may average around 1,294 MMCFE per day during the second half of the year, based on its revised guidance midpoint. This would be a 3% increase from its 1H 2025 average production.

The NYMEX natural gas strip for 2H 2025 is now around $3.40, and Comstock is projected to generate $763 million in 2H 2025 revenues after hedges.

Type

Barrels/Mcf

Realized $ Per Barrel/Mcf

Revenue ($ Million)

Oil (Barrels)

25,000

$60.00

Natural Gas [MCF]

237,946,000

Total

Comstock is running eight rigs during 2H 2025, including four on its legacy Haynesville acreage and four in the Western Haynesville. This is up one rig on its legacy Haynesville acreage.

Despite the increase, Comstock expects roughly flat capex compared to 1H 2025 as it also is planning for slightly less completion activity during the second half of the year.

$ Million

CapEx and Leasing

Total Expenses

Comstock is thus projected to have around $67 million in cash burn during the second half of 2025.

Notes On Debt

That additional cash burn would increase Comstock's debt up to around $3.1 billion. Comstock still has plenty of credit facility borrowing capacity remaining though and should also be able to keep its leverage ratio below 3.5x unless NYMEX natural gas prices averaged in the mid-$2 range or lower.

Current 2026 strip prices are around $4, which should allow Comstock to generate a modest amount of positive free cash flow next year and potentially lower its leverage to around 2.2x to 2.3x by the end of 2026 instead.

Comstock is exploring non-core asset sales to help it deleverage as well.

Notes On Valuation

I've reduced my estimate of Comstock's value from $14.50 per share to $13.50 per share at long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

This is partly due to weaker near-term strip prices that have reduced Comstock's projected 2026 free cash flow by close to $100 million since I last looked at it.

As well, Comstock's downward production guidance revision also negatively affects its value despite its progress in de-risking the Western Haynesville.

I thought Comstock was greatly overvalued before, but it seems to be getting to around a more reasonable level now. At long-term $4.00 NYMEX natural gas instead, I'd value it at around $17.50 per share, although that's a bit more optimistic than my typical view.

Conclusion

Comstock is now expected to have close to $150 million in 2025 cash burn due to weaker near-term natural gas prices and a downward revision in its production guidance (resulting in expectations for a double-digit decline in year-over-year production).

Comstock continues to make progress with de-risking the Western Haynesville, although its legacy Haynesville assets are still more capital efficient. With its cash burn and weaker production expectations, I have reduced its estimated value to $13.50 per share in my base case scenario for long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: More Yield For A Little More Risk: Yields Up To 15%
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

When evaluating a sector that has experienced an uptick in volatility, the question becomes, what path should I approach it from to have the best potential outcome for my investment perspective and philosophy?

The MBS (Mortgage-Backed Security) sector is considered to be an extremely low-risk sector because of the government guarantee, especially on agency MBS. This means that companies that invest in this type of asset can utilize a large amount of leverage when secured by the MBS, as the MBS itself is considered extremely low-risk. Various options are available in the market to gain exposure to MBS if desired.

We've previously highlighted an ETF that invests directly in MBS with no leverage and no frills and yields about 6%. It is a great option for an investor looking for a low-risk route, but what if you want to get a little bit more risk from the sector?

Today, I want to look at two ways you can boost your yield, but also accept a little bit more risk in the process. The first option will give you over double the yield of the ETF. The second is only a couple of percentage points of yield.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: AGNC – Yield 15.3%

AGNC Investment Corporation (AGNC) is a mortgage REIT that invests primarily in MBS. AGNC mixes in leverage to boost returns and income generated from what is overwhelmingly considered an ultra-low-risk investment type. AGNC saw its tangible book value dip to $7.81/share, down about 5.4% from Q1:

However, in their earnings call in Q1, management noted that book value was 7.5%-8% lower as of April 21st, implying a book value of $7.59-$7.63. The driver of book value is influenced both by the price of agency MBS and by the relationship between MBS prices and hedging prices. AGNC hedges by using interest rate swaps and shorting US Treasuries. These are natural hedges because MBS and Treasury prices have a fairly strong correlation. However, a strong correlation does not mean they move in lockstep.

Here is the current coupon MBS spread to US Treasuries and to Interest Rate Swaps: Source

The spread to Treasuries increased only 3 bps. The spread to swaps increased 11 bps. Every quarter, AGNC reports its sensitivity to "basis risk", which is the change in the relationship between MBS and hedges.

Note that a 10 bps increase in spread is anticipated to cause a 5.2% decline in NAV. It was the same in Q1; we saw an 11 bps increase in spread to swaps, which is their primary hedge position right now, and NAV declined 5.3%.

What does this mean in practical terms? Well, as is the case for all financial transactions, lower prices are good for buyers. The spread between MBS and hedging costs is literally how AGNC profits. They buy MBS, they buy hedges, and they profit from the difference. The higher the spread, the higher the profits are for any new positions implemented today. That means older positions bought at lower spreads are less valuable, because you can get a higher return from new purchases.

If we go back to a time when AGNC was trading for $20, like Q4 2017, this spread was 60-70 bps. It's an irony of bonds that the worse the forward return, the higher the prices. The better the forward return, the lower the prices.

AGNC is stepping up as a buyer, adding an additional $3 billion in assets in Q2:

It was able to do this while keeping leverage relatively flat at 7.6x (vs. 7.5x in Q1) by issuing common equity. AGNC's share price traded at a substantial premium to NAV throughout the quarter, which allows it to issue equity freely. In recent years, there have been many investors who have relied on book value as the primary determinant of when to buy or sell agency mREITs. And over the past decade or so, this has been an effective strategy. However, it is noteworthy that during the periods where agency mREITs have outperformed the most, like during the GFC, they traded at very large premiums, and someone looking at book value would have sat on the sidelines.

This quarter, AGNC reported that its book value is well below the trading price, and the market apparently doesn't care—choosing instead to focus on the high cash flow and the forward ROE (return on equity) being about 19%, which is a level we usually see during periods of market panic. It is very rare to have such a high ROE while also having an environment where AGNC can issue equity and buy billions as fast as they can, because usually these high prices coincide with destabilization in the financial markets.

Turning to cash flow, we saw a slight dip in earnings to $0.38/share.

We noted this was likely to happen at last quarter's earnings writing:

"The bottom line is that AGNC's $0.44 in earnings amply covers its $0.36 dividend. We might see a little bit of a dip in earnings based on the timing of interest rate swap maturities and how quickly AGNC can keep increasing its asset yield."

AGNC has swaps that they took out in 2020-2021 that are maturing, and this is gradually increasing their cost of funds. So we expect variation quarter to quarter with earnings, due to the lumpiness of those maturities. We continue to believe that Q4 2024 represents a bottom and that earnings will generally trend upward; it just won't be in a straight line.

We expect AGNC to continue issuing equity as long as they are trading at a premium and will continue to make its portfolio larger. In the earnings call, management was cognizant of the fact that its growth had a practical limit. They want to make sure that their scale doesn't become so large that they have problems transacting in the hedging markets at the volumes they need. However, there is ample room for growth, and the longer that interest rates remain in this gray zone where they aren't really going up, but nobody really knows when they will come down, AGNC has more time to buy higher coupons. That will be a significant positive when the day comes that the equity market falls and the "flight to safety" takes place.

Pick #2 ARR-C – Yield 8%

ARMOUR Residential REIT (ARR) is a mortgage REIT that invests primarily in MBS as well as Treasury notes. ARR is more readily considered a peer to Dynex Capital (DX) than much larger mREITs like AGNC or Annaly (NLY). As such, management's ability to navigate different sector changes greatly impacts its ability to provide strong returns. When we compare ARR common equity returns to those of DX, for example, we can see that the management's ability to navigate and the choices they make have vastly different outcomes:

Historically, ARR has been willing to issue shares when they're trading at a premium and buy back shares when they are trading at a discount to book value. This can make the number of outstanding shares confusing because in the same quarter, they may buy back and sell shares. It also means that ARR's management team is more willing than other management teams to cut or raise the dividend as they feel necessary. This makes, as far as our personal perspective, ARR's common equity unattractive.

If we climb one step higher in the capital stack, we can find a persistently attractive opportunity in the preferred shares that are outstanding.

ARMOUR Residential REIT, 7.00% Series C Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (ARR.PR.C) offers a strong 8% yielding monthly dividend-paying preferred security. ARR-C is currently trading post-call but is highly unlikely to be called in the current interest rate environment. ARR-C's dividend was covered 22x by its distributable earnings this past quarter, which is consistent with the prior year's coverage as well.

ARR-C is a beneficiary of management's ability to continue to sell new common shares to the market, even if those common shares are not providing strong total returns to their shareholders. The coverage of the preferred dividend remains strong and consistently strong, only taking about $3 million per quarter to payout, while ARR is generating in excess of $67 million of distributable earnings.

If ARR-C is unexpectedly called, you would benefit from its current discount to PAR. This, combined with a stable and attractive monthly-paid yield, makes ARR-C a highly attractive, lower-risk avenue to collect strong income from MBS exposure.

Conclusion

By accepting just a little bit more risk, an investor can collect upwards of 16% or 8% yields versus the available 6% yield on traditional MBS at this time. By using ARR-C to collect 8%, an investor can unlock more income without taking on any increased level of significant risk. If an investor wants 16% yields that are paid out monthly from AGNC, they can readily collect that and still gain valuable exposure to the MBS sector. It is a sector that is getting ready to heat up and provide even better total returns, regardless of the avenue in which you decide to have exposure.

When it comes to retirement, matching the yield you'll need for your financial future is important. If you are a well-to-do investor who does not want to collect high levels of income because you're more risk-averse, then ARR-C may be perfect for you. A simple 8% yield without a lot of risk and not a lot of frills. However, if you're an investor who wants more income per dollar invested, AGNC can provide you with a wonderful route to collect high-yield income on a regular basis.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Alphabet: Why An Antitrust Breakup Is Good
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) defeat in antitrust court and perceived threat from LLMs to its search engine ad revenue fuel perceptions of an existential crisis. Our view is that, if anything, the rise of LLMs is positive to all of Alphabet's business lines, including search ad revenue. Regarding the DOJ case, all outcomes are positive at this point.

Valuation

Among the magnificent seven, Alphabet has the lowest valuation ratios across all metrics, on both the TTM and Forward basis, except for EV/Sales, where it's second to Amazon (AMZN), only because of the retail giant's razor-thin margins, which justifiably limit what investors are willing to pay per unit of sales.

GOOG might appear overvalued when compared to the Communications sector (its official GICS industry categorization since a 2018 overhaul), as mirrored in Seeking Alpha's Quant Score.

However, when compared to the broader Information Technology market, GOOG is undervalued despite its tech moat, leadership, and brand, as shown in the table below.

Perhaps more telling is that GOOG now trades at a discount to its own historical valuation. For example, GOOG's current PE ratio is about 21% lower than its 5-year average.

Antitrust Issues: Bad News is Good News

Alphabet lost a critical antitrust case in August 2024, and the ramifications could be more serious than anything we've witnessed before.

Until now, the US and EU prosecutors have been content with fines and cease and desist orders, and voluntary changes to business practices.

However, in May 2025, in relation to the August 2024 case, the DOJ proposed breaking up the company, particularly spinning off Android OS, Chrome Browser, Google Ad Exchange "AdX", among other business lines. A Judge is set to determine the appropriate remedies/sentence this month. Investors should brace for some volatility.

Our view is that a "negative" court outcome, i.e, a splitting of Alphabet into smaller companies, is a positive outcome to shareholders, as breaking down the company will unlock value to shareholders, who will receive shares in these said companies. The higher multiples assigned to these newly formed companies will more than offset the negative impact of the loss of synergies on net income.

Take, for example, AppLovin (APP), whose business model aligns with Google AdMob, trades at a forward PE ratio of 48x, about twice that of Alphabet's consolidated PE. Similarly, The Trade Desk (TTD), which competes with Google DV 360 service, has a forward PE of 48x, highlighting the weight that Alphabet's legal issues are putting on its valuation. Magnite (MGNI), which runs the largest independent Ad exchange, also has a forward PE ratio about 36% above that of Alphabet's consolidated PE ratio.

Waymoo, as the #1 autonomous driving technology provider, could also achieve high valuation as a standalone company, despite being unprofitable.

Another example is Snowflake (SNOW), which is smaller and growing at a slower rate than Google Cloud, yet has a forward PE of 200x.

Gemini and YouTube are growing at spectacular rates, and would also attract investors' enthusiasm as standalone companies, unlocking value if Alphabet is broken down.

Alphabet's Tensor Processing Units "TPUs" which are currently used in Alphabet's data centers, could also be turned into a standalone business. Based on the current valuations of fabless semiconductor companies, Google TPU could attract investors' attention, translating to meaningful capital gains to Alphabet's shareholders if it were spun off.

One can argue that the harsher the judge's upcoming sentencing, the better the outcome for investors. Sure, there will be some synergies lost, resulting in slightly lower net income, but this will be offset by higher valuation multiples, in my view.

LLMs Threat Overrated

There are concerns that the rise of generative AI and LLMs will negatively impact Google Search's Ads business. However, Q2'25 results don't seem to support this premise. Google Search revenue rose by 11.7%, in line with the historical average, notwithstanding the COVID pandemic disruptions.

The launch of Search Generative Experience "SGE" resulted in more frequent and deeper interactions with Google Search, according to Philipp Schindler, Alphabet's CBO. This also offsets the lost profiling opportunity from informational queries shifting to other LLMs.

Overall, looking at revenue by segment, I think that generative AI has a positive impact on Alphabet's businesses.

Q2 2024 Q2 2025 YoY Change
Google Search & other 48,509 54,190 12%
YouTube ads 8,663 9,796 13%
Google Network 7,444 7,354 -1%
Google advertising 64,616 71,340 10%
Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices 9,312 11,203 20%
Google Services total 73,928 82,543 12%
Google Cloud 10,347 13,624 32%
Other Bets 365 373 2%
Total revenues 84,742 96,428 14%

Dividends and Capital Allocation

Another concern facing Alphabet is its CapEx spending. During the Q2 2025 earnings call, the company noted that it is raising its CapEx this year to $85 billion, up $10 from the previous guidance, which represents almost a 60% increase from last year's budget.

In my view, the company didn't do itself any favors when it started a dividend program last year, given the CapEx requirement as the company navigates the emerging AI/ML landscape.

The good news is that the company's cash spending and capital returns are almost fully covered by operating cash flows.

Here's the company's capital allocation in the TTM ended June 2025

TTM June 2025 (billion $)
Operating Cash Flow $133.7
CapEx $66.9
Share Buybacks $59.5
Dividends $9.9
Retained Cash -$2.6

Risks

I think that the primary risk facing Alphabet is that fears over its market position become a self-fulfilling prophecy by impacting its ability to attract and maintain talent. Meta's (META) recruiting spree demonstrates the importance of talent in maintaining technological and, by extension, market position. If job-seekers perceive Alphabet's future or technological position as uncertain, they might opt for other companies with clearer career paths, creating a vicious cycle of poor recruiting apparatus, which, in turn undermines market position, further determining talent recruitment.

Summary

Alphabet's ability to grow its revenue and profits despite the changing competitive and technological landscape highlights its agility and solid portfolio positioning in the AI/ML era.

This is not the first time that the company has experienced technological and regulatory change. The present panic reminds me of Alphabet in the mid-2000s, when social media companies started introducing targeted ad services that leveraged users' data, yet Google Search persevered and prospered. Generative AI and LLMs are accelerating digitalization trends, the same tailwinds that helped the company deliver extraordinary results for the past two decades.

Finally, any outcome from the antitrust court judge is a positive one, in my view. Alphabet has demonstrated its ability to navigate cease and desist orders by adjusting its business practices while maintaining growth. If the worst happens, and the company is ordered to spin-off some of its subsidiaries, current investors are in for some lucrative share dividends in the new entities. Finally, the court sentence/remedy will remove the uncertainty that has been weighing on the company's valuation.

data.title: Civitas Resources: Cash Flowing, Completely Mispriced
data.description:
Let’s cut to the chase: Civitas Resources (NYSE: NYSE:CIVI) is one of those hidden gems. It’s a profitable, capital-disciplined oil producer with some of the best acreage in the Lower 48, a strong hedge book, and clear visibility into production growth in the second half of the year. Despite all that, the stock trades at just 5x forward earnings, yields almost 7%, and sits at a ridiculous 58% discount to book.

This is one of those setups where the market’s narrative and the company’s fundamentals are completely disconnected. And that disconnect is what makes CIVI one of the most compelling value names in the energy space right now.

Valuation

Here’s where things get interesting. CIVI trades around $30/share while its tangible book value is closer to $71. That’s a 58% discount to what the assets are worth on paper.

From an earnings perspective, CIVI is dirt cheap. They posted $171M in adjusted free cash flow in 1Q25 alone. Annualize that and you’re at nearly $700M, which easily covers the base dividend ($2.00/share or 7% yield) and still leaves room for buybacks and debt paydown.

You’re getting all this for just 5x earnings.

The best part about CIVI is that it doesn’t need high oil prices to make the math work. They’ve built a business that stays FCF positive even at $40 WTI. And if you include the dividend, breakeven sits around $45. That’s what happens when you control costs, own low-decline assets, and run a tight ship operationally.

Management’s done a good job locking in margins too. About 50% of their crude volumes are hedged for the rest of 2025 at an average floor of $68/barrel. They also have strong natural gas hedging in place, including Waha and CIG basis protection. In other words, they’re not just crossing fingers and praying for $85 oil.

On top of that, they’re implementing a $100M cost optimization program, $40M of that is expected to hit this year. Some of that is coming from faster drill times (10% faster in the Permian Basin), better completions (Simulfrac throughput up 5% QoQ), and using more local sand in the DJ (now over 90%, up from 50%). This matters, and it’s already showing up in the numbers.

Debt Is High, But It’s Being Handled

CIVI does have a bit of leverage, with net debt sitting at $5.1B as of Q1. But the goal is to get that down to $4.5B by year-end, and they’re on track to do it. The balance sheet is actually in decent shape, no near-term maturities, strong liquidity ($1.5B), and a flexible capital program that can adjust to the macro.

They’re planning to raise $300M through asset sales (not core E&P assets — think infrastructure, surface rights, non-producing land). That’ll help hit the debt target without fire-selling anything important.

Plus, the company tapped the debt markets in May with a $500M senior notes offering, using the proceeds to reduce revolver borrowings. That gives them even more flexibility going forward.

Production Dips Were Temporary

Q1 production was a little soft, coming in at 311k Boe/d (with 141k Bbl/d of oil), thanks to a lull in DJ Basin completions and some weather issues. But that’s already turning around. Management is guiding for Q2 oil volumes to jump 5% and total production to land between 320 - 328k boe/d, with most of the growth coming from the Permian.

The Permian Basin is where the action is. In Q1, 42% of their Permian wells were in the Delaware Basin - their highest-return zone - and early results have been strong. Drill times beat forecasts by 10%, and Simulfrac operations in the Midland Basin continue to gain efficiency. The second quarter will see the most activity of the year, setting up a production ramp in Q3 and Q4.

As for the DJ, that’s stabilizing. After going several months with almost no new wells turned in line, activity is finally resuming. Completions are happening faster and cheaper than expected, and management reaffirmed full-year guidance.

What The Market Is Missing?

CIVI’s cheap for a few reasons. None of them really tied to how the business is actually performing.

First off, the company’s still a bit of a new face. It was built through roll-ups in the DJ and Permian, so some investors might be treating it like a “wait and see” story. That skepticism lingers, even though the integration’s gone smoothly and the assets are delivering.

Second, there’s a DJ Basin bias. Investors hear “Colorado” and think headaches, permitting delays, regulation. But CIVI’s not chasing fringe acreage. Most of their DJ footprint is in areas where activity is steady and approvals are routine. The market just hasn’t updated that mental model yet.

Then there’s the macro. Oil prices have been choppy, capital’s rotated out of small-cap energy, and a lot of generalist funds just aren’t paying attention to names like this. Add in $5B in net debt and people assume it’s overlevered, even though the maturities are years out, and the plan to get that to $4.5B by year-end is well underway.

Shareholders Are Getting Paid

Despite the macro noise, CIVI is still returning capital. In Q1, they paid $50M in dividends and bought back $71M worth of stock (1.5M shares). That’s nearly 2% of the float gone in one quarter. Once they hit the $4.5B net debt target, expect buybacks to pick up again.

The $2.00/share annual base dividend looks safe too. Even at $55 oil, the business remains free cash flow positive. Management was clear on the call: the dividend is not in jeopardy, and they’re not planning to cut it even if prices weaken. It’s fully covered by cash flow and hedges.

Risks

Every upstream oil company carries some risk, especially in today’s market. Oil prices could drop further. Service costs could stay sticky. Regulatory headaches in the DJ Basin could flare up again. And yeah, asset sales might get delayed if the bid-ask spread doesn’t close.

But here’s the thing: all of that is already priced in. You’re paying 5x earnings and getting a 7% yield while you wait.

The balance sheet isn’t pristine, but it’s getting better. And the downside is cushioned by hedges, cost cuts, and capital discipline. There’s no need for heroic oil prices for the stock to work, just some time.

My Take

CIVI is doing almost everything right. They’re focused on capital returns, cutting costs, running an efficient operation, and sticking to the plan. And yet, the stock price doesn’t reflect this. This is a healthy business with a strong base, upside torque if oil moves, and robust margin of safety.

If you believe oil stays above $60, this stock is a layup. If oil goes back to $70+, it’s a home run. But even in a mid-$50s world, CIVI is just deeply mispriced.

This isn’t some speculative shale bet. It’s a cash-flowing machine priced like it’s circling the drain. And that kind of mispricing doesn’t last forever.

data.title: Technical Note: Time For A Turn
data.description:
Macro Perspective

Our April 7th Buy reflected pronounced fear and heavy selling, suggesting a change of ownership that typically comes with market bottoms. Today, 32.9% higher, SPX is in a very different position. News that Courts are reviewing President Trump’s tariffs could signal a potentially serious problem for the market. Recall in April, tariffs were disallowed by the Courts ( Tariff Risks), but the Administration applied a 150 day workaround while the legal standing was reconsidered. That day has come and could threaten all the significant wins that President Trump has achieved. Should this happen, which does not seem an unrealistic outcome of the review, we suspect that the market would weigh in with its displeasure. Now that SPX has hit all-time new highs, we think this represents a significant new risk for equity investors.

Tariffs Announced this Year

Tariffs post-WWII were set around 2.5% allowing Allies to recover economically but were never normalized in 75 years(!) – New levels reflect closer to a level playing field – This is a major positive for the US economy!

If these tariff adjustments are reversed, even with likely workarounds and a trip to the Supreme Court, the markets would likely suffer significant uncertainties that would probably cause multiples and valuations to decline from recent all-time highs. We view this and what we see as a parade of concerning economic data as a serious risk to stock valuations.

Recession Watch

Panic over tariffs that investors (still) don’t understand have given way to a level of optimism that may not be supported by the current state of the economy.

Consumer Angst Is Real

Student loans are impacting large numbers of Gen Z and Millennials (Student Loans) The resumption of payments and concomitant hit to credit scores are causing young people to cut spending to pay off loans. We believe this reduction of spending by one of the most active demographic segments will have meaningful implications on retail sales and GDP. Even high-end retail is struggling, suggesting wider problems for consumers (High-end Weakness). Housing costs are the highest in many years, hurting young people trying to reach for the American dream (Housing Costs). The fact that food purchases are increasingly on Buy-Now-Pay_Later (BNPL) suggests a deeper problem for consumers, something that shows up across the board (P&G Sees Weakness).

S&P500 Weekly Supply/Demand Chart

Source: Summit Analytic Partners, LLC

SPX is now on the brink of a significant new Sell Signal

Volume-Adjusted Price Weekly Chart

Momentum (green) is max bullish – VAP (red) may be done as well

Tech may have formed a top in a diagonal wedge pattern – it brokedown and retested. NVDA is oversold now but could easily retrace the entire bull leg preceding this pattern back to $40 in weeks to months. A near term rally (bounce) seems likely with EPS coming soon and continuing AI investment

NVDA Weekly Supply/Demand Chart

Tech is about to start a new Sell Signal – Volatility is the lowest seen in a long time!

Volume-Adjusted Price Weekly Chart

Momentum (green) is approaching key resistance – VAP (red) may have topped

Euro/Dollar Weekly Supply/Demand Chart

The dollar may be poised for a larger rally soon after a near-term correction

Volume-Adjusted Price Weekly Chart

Momentum and VAP are both falling but not bottoming yet suggesting dollar strength will return soon

BTC Weekly Supply/Demand Chart

BTC may be poised for a larger correction soon

Volume-Adjusted Price Weekly Chart

Momentum is now falling and VAP is near resistance – not conclusive yet but cause for concern

Risks

Liquidity from the Election last year has not yet hit in terms of inflation, though it may be starting. Past surges of government money printing resulted in inflation after 9-18 months. One year ago, the Biden Administration commenced the largest, fastest surge in printed money on record (Money Surge). The economy has not yet seen the inevitable impact of this profligate surge in fake money. History shows, as it did in 1H23, that big surges in deficit spending result in new inflationary pressures down the line. We expect inflation to accelerate in 2H25 as a result, something that negatively impacts long-duration asset (think tech stocks!) valuations.

We have long been skeptics about government jobs and inflation data calculus. ShadowStats.com shows that true inflation (calculated as it was in 1980 before Greenspan’s many ‘adjustments’) runs more than double reported CPI. The same has proven true of jobs numbers (Jobs Revisions. The lack of accurate data makes market watchers’ jobs more difficult, but not impossible. We acknowledge that another surge of liquidity from printing presses could extend the current bull cycle. It is a constant risk for forecasting, especially in the last 25 years as government deficits accelerated. If inflation doesn’t show up until later it could push the pattern out into the future towards the 18-months end of the range (year end?).

The Trade

We believe that SPX and tech stocks like NVDA are poised to correct soon. Since this pattern is basically a binary play, if we are wrong then we would reverse quickly and re-establish longs for the short run, but would continue to watch for a downturn that we believe is likely ahead. SPX may have topped at 6427, but we worry that RSI divergences have not fully developed, which could presage another new high in the near term. Either way we would use any strength to exit longs and establish shorts. The magnitude of the volatility creates a significant opportunity for investors/traders to profit from anticipating negative economic/political news.

Conclusion

We believe that stocks are ready to correct after an almost 33% run since April lows. One cannot print money indefinitely without having to face the consequences. We expect payback to be painful and drawn out based on the amount of debt accumulated without much productive investment to show for it. Its been fun on the long-side this spring and summer, but see accumulating risks to the runup in stock valuations.

Additional Information Available Upon Request

data.title: Teleperformance: Navigating Challenges
data.description:
Today is not an ideal day to provide a comprehensive analysis of Teleperformance's (OTCPK:TLPFF) (OTCPK:TLPFY) performance. However, we will use this challenging downturn as an opportunity to share our perspective on the company. Having been at the heart of discussions surrounding AI disruption in the customer experience industry for several years, Teleperformance seized the opportunity to unveil a new strategic plan, set mid-term objectives, and establish a capital allocation framework centered around AI. Even if we reported a positive stance on the recent 'Future Forward' strategy, and Teleperformance demonstrated a commitment to advancing its AI investments and deployment, the market view remains essentially unchanged. Given the rapid pace of transformation, Wall Street's skepticism is evident, with shares down 30% since the announcement and over 80% from their peak, now trading at a sub-5x PE ratio.

We will revisit the discussion on AI upside later. Still, three key factors supported our buy rating: 1) the growth of the Specialized Services segment with an increased EBITDA contribution, 2) Majorel's value-creating acquisition, and 3) a double-digit free cash flow yield with downside protection, underpinned by a favorable shareholders' remuneration policy.

Fig 1

Q2 Update and Our Take

The company reported Q2 organic growth of +1.3% on a yearly basis, with a miss to the consensus expectation of +1.7%. Trends diverged significantly by division. In numbers, the company's growth was primarily driven by the Core Services segment, which accelerated to +3.5%. In contrast, the Specialized Services division underperformed, with revenue declining 11.6%. Here at the Lab, we anticipated this negative sales evolution, and in our last analysis, in our risk section, we reported the negative effect of the non-renewal of the TLS contract. Still related to the segment, we report lower sales growth from LanguageLine Solutions in the US. Going down to the P&L, while some cost adjustments helped stabilize margins in Q2, the segment's overall contribution to group performance declined. The group EBITA margin reached 13.6% on a reported basis and remained stable at 13.9% excluding FX. Looking at the net profit, TP reported a net income of €249 million, which decreased by -14.4% year-over-year.

  1. (Still a positive view on the AI) From our extensive analysis on AI tools and large language models (LLM), we have found that training AI models on video and recorded data is highly beneficial. With Teleperformance’s extensive collection of recorded customer service calls, the company might be well-positioned to leverage this data. The company might reduce headcount costs and improve its margins with a full AI internal service. This without reducing its quality of service. The company also invests in startups (and new partnerships), allocating 20% of its FCF toward technology initiatives. In our last analysis, we concluded with the following: "There appear to be two opposing views on GenAI’s impact on the customer experience industry: one suggesting it undermines the very foundation of the CX business model, and another viewing it as a new tool that companies in the sector will integrate, making the recent derating unjustified. We align with the latter perspective."
  2. (Core Services sales acceleration - positive) Another supportive evidence that is still related to AI disruption debates is the Core Services evolution. As a reminder, this segment represents 70% of the company's profits, and lower sales have been the main drag in recent years (at least when compared to Specialized Services). Therefore, we positively view the +3.5% growth acceleration organically in Q2 (Fig. 2). These results were a broad-based beat across regions with Americas, EMEA, and APAC at +1% and +5.7% (Fig. 3).
  3. (Lower Outlook - negative) The EBITA margins reached 13.6%, albeit with a -30 basis points drag from FX translation. The company has tweaked its 2025 outlook lower (Fig. 4) with sales growth expected at the low end of the +2-4% range and margins to be +0-10bps to 15.0-15.1% at Constant Exchange Rate, not on a reported basis. Still, on the positive, the FCF is still guided at approximately €1 billion;
  4. (Upside in shareholders' remuneration—positive) With an unchanged FCF, the company trades with an FCF yield of 25% and a dividend yield of 6%. Given the revenue and blue-chip contracts, we believe the company will announce a buyback soon.

Source: Teleperformance Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

From a fundamental perspective, we maintain a buy rating; however, the market, as always, is signaling a lack of approval for Teleperformance at this point. We must adapt accordingly. While there are indications of Core Services momentum, they are likely to be overshadowed in the short term by the significant shortfall in Specialized Services and the lack of clarity regarding any potential recovery. With the current FX, we lower Teleperformance sales from €10.4 billion to €10.2 billion. Still, the company notes that the significant investment in the past 12 months bears fruit, and we should see sales increase in H2. As already reported, the loss of the UK visa contract is well-known, and we expect a -€50 million drag in EBITDA in Q3. After cutting Specialized organic growth, we see EBITA margin pressure, and considering the FCX impact, we now anticipate a group margin estimate of 14.6% (which is below TP's outlook). This represents an EBITA of €1.48 billion. Going down to the P&L forecast, we report that TP reported a higher-than-anticipated tax rate. The press release report "effective tax rate of 33.1%, in line with the expected full-year trajectory." With €200 million in interest expenses, our net profit reached €844 million. At the current outstanding shares, we derive an EPS of €14.01 (from a previous estimate of €15.70).

Going to the valuation, we are not looking backward and no longer applying a P/E target of 13x. Still, we need to report a FCF yield above 20%. We believe Concentrix is a good trading comps that provides a reference on a FWD EV/EBITDA of 5.5x and P/E of 10x. We also report that H1 net debt increased, mainly due to their investments. The good thing is that cash flows are expected to rise in H2, supporting a lower net debt evolution (€4.5 billion). So, blending the valuation methodology (PE: target price €140 per share / EV/EBITDA: target price of €65 per share), we derive a target price of €102.5 per share ($59 in ADR).

Risks

Teleperformance downside risks include: 1) AI disruption, 2) lower sales in the Specialized Services division, 3) incumbent AI players, 4) value disruption M&A with failure to integrate new technologies, 5) FX changes as reported in Fig 5, 6) wage inflation, 7) restructuring initiatives due to subdued margin, 8) lower target price, and negative earnings revision, and 9) regulatory changes. Teleperformance is subject to strict U.S. regulations regarding the "Do Not Call List." In Q2, the company was also impacted by a higher tax rate (33% vs a French rate of 25%).

Fig 5

Conclusion

Many of the company's headwinds were anticipated. The stock is trading below €70, down 20% from the previous day. While the underlying fundamentals remain mostly intact, we still consider this a buy, although significant technology risks persist. We view the company as a potential AI beneficiary, but we believe we are among the few who see it this way.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: First Majestic Silver: Valuation Getting Ahead Of Itself
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

First Majestic Silver (NYSE:AG) is a silver and gold mining company (57% of revenue from silver, 33% from gold, 10% from others) with 4 operating mines in Mexico (and 2 in care and maintenance), being the only public mining company with its own minting facility. Overall, the stock has been lagging the price of the commodity and only started properly following it again back in June. Still, we’re talking about a stock that’s historically coming at a beta to silver of 3x, which is only up ~30% compared to the levels from a year ago, almost identical to the commodity. As miners are generally a way to get leverage on the price of their commodities and the company is starting to show impressive results, let’s see if this might be an opportunity.

Following higher metal prices, the company’s cash flow increased significantly, being poised to continue improving if metal prices keep staying at record levels. On top of that, the recent acquisition of Gatos Silver announced earlier this year helped them increase production significantly, with increased guidance despite weather-related issues and power outages in June. The company even provided a useful summary of their mining operations, justifying the better-than-expected results:

Operational performance at Santa Elena and San Dimas continues to meet or exceed expectations, allowing us to capitalize on the favourable metal price environment. While La Encantada had a slower start to the year, we anticipate a stronger second half.

This comes as their Q2 silver equivalent production went up by 48% YoY, thanks to a 76% surge in attributable silver output, of which 1.5 million oz came from the newly purchased Los Gatos mine and a 17% production increase at San Dimas. Now, they expect 14.8 million to 15.8 million ounces of silver and 135,000 to 144,000 ounces of gold, meaning an increase of 6% and 2%, respectively, compared to the midpoint of the previous guidance.

This comes even with a higher CAPEX of $193 million expected this year, with a lot of that going into development and exploration, with $117 of it being allocated for expansion, while $76 million is for sustaining operations. This also provides a good idea of how much this can be cut if the company really has to go into a “survival mode” in case commodity prices fall (around $100 million with minimal investments).

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a great position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities and long-term debt. Overall, we are left with $2.83 billion in equity on a $3.92 billion market cap, which is not bad for silver miners in today’s market, especially since the balance sheet is backward-looking and does not reflect the present value of their assets, which are likely significantly higher after the jumps we’ve seen in precious metals prices.

Dividends & Buybacks

Since the company’s cash flow was negative until last year as a result of the higher CAPEX, their dividend has been relatively meaningless. However, now, with a higher production, lower costs, and finally solid free cash flows, we might see AG be able to pay better yields. I don’t think this should be a focus now, however, as I’d much rather see them reinvest in the company than pay a 1-2% dividend just to be in line with the market, as they are not able to sustainably pay significant yields at this Price/FCF ratio.

Risks & Opportunities

I see that somewhat of an issue in today’s market is the dilution. However, keep in mind that this dilution isn’t used for things like paying a dividend, which would indeed be a waste, but rather for acquisitions such as Gatos Silver (for 190 million AG shares). This is common in the mining industry, as these companies often don’t have enough cash for such deals, and getting $970 million in debt to fund the Gatos deal would’ve realistically been a terrible decision. Looking into it, AG was valued at ~44.4x Price/TTM FCF, while Gatos came at ~18.4x, meaning they should see a significant increase in the cash flow per share in the near future, on top of “numerous synergies and opportunities already being identified and leveraged,” which can add value on top, plus any future discoveries coming as a bonus. Also, the timing of this purchase is definitely impressive, getting it right as silver prices started jumping on top of their own silver output declining.

As I mentioned, the company expects 14.8 million to 15.8 million ounces of silver in 2025 now after the upward revision, but if we look into it, their 70% stake in Los Gatos is actually their largest silver producer now, even at the lowest point of expectations. It’s the 2nd if we look at silver equivalent ounces since it produces a lot less gold, but this also solidifies their exposure to silver in particular.

However, keep in mind that AG has been constantly issuing shares over time. Again, negative cash flows over the last years due to higher CAPEX means they have to get that capital somehow, and they seem to prefer this over getting debt, which would’ve also generated interest and even lower cash flows on top of increasing their overall risk.

Regarding discoveries, they recently announced their second gold-silver discovery in a year at the Santa Elena site in Mexico, which could add to their long-term pipeline and provide potential to at least stabilize the mine’s production for longer and expand its lifetime.

Still, a very real risk to keep in mind is their exposure to Mexico (the largest silver producer), as mentioned in other silver miner analyses posted before. This means that any political or economic issues, especially as a result of Trump’s tariffs, could potentially affect the company even if they were not directly targeted. Weakening Mexico’s economy could lead to workforce/tax issues for First Majestic, while tariffs can lead to supply chain issues or other types of bottlenecks, especially if Mexico retaliates in any way (such as retaliatory tariffs making mining equipment from the US more expensive). I believe tariffs are more of a near-term way to get leverage for deal negotiations in the US’ favor rather than a permanent thing, as their inflationary effects could not be great for the country - and if they are a long-term thing, precious metals should continue to go up.

In fact, First Majestic is currently in a tax dispute with Mexico. We’re talking about a disagreement over how tax obligations should be calculated, with First Majestic relying on a previously agreed-upon Advance Pricing Agreement (aka an APA) from 2012, which Mexico later nullified in court (currently appealed by AG), asserting that the company underpaid taxes by allegedly manipulating silver prices through this APA. The case is ongoing and involves both domestic Mexican legal battles and international arbitration, and more information can be found in their presentations (page 38 here) and the media. For now, it remains an uncertainty and potential liability to keep in mind.

Regardless, as First Majestic says, there’s no substitute for silver, as it is quite a fundamental and even future-facing commodity, as almost 60% of its demand comes from industrial uses like electronics, solar panels, alloys, and more. Only about 15-20% goes into jewelry and investing/banks, respectively. What’s interesting is the way these sources of demand have evolved over the past decade. While jewelry demand remained almost identical over the past decade, investment fell nearly 33%, but industrial uses went up significantly thanks especially to electronics, which went up 78.3% during this time and especially photovoltaics, up nearly 290% during this time, becoming the second highest source of demand for silver, surpassing investment and jewelry!

This is a very important development that probably came as a result of the production costs of photovoltaics falling by about 90% over the past decade. However, an important aspect to consider is that this could result in China accounting for an even higher amount of demand for silver, meaning that potential issues in China can result in issues for the demand, similar to copper, iron, and a majority of other commodities.

Also importantly, in 2024, China’s silver demand grew by a whopping 7%, and that’s before the country’s policy shift and economic measures meant to stimulate the economy were announced for 2025. This means that silver is much more tied to how the economy is doing compared to gold, whose value is much more about the market’s sentiment at the time, which I believe is also a reason why silver lagged gold’s jump for so long. Back in April, market sentiment was almost at Extreme Fear levels based on the Fear and Greed index, which likely amplified silver’s store of value demand.

The low investment demand came at a time when gold went up significantly, while silver prices lagged it severely - until 2025, when it jumped 34% YTD. During bad/uncertain times for the economy (i.e., tariffs, potential trade war escalating, real wars, recessions, etc.), we might see a shift in this demand and a higher focus on silver from investment sources, similar to what happened to gold. But, as I keep saying in other precious metals-related stock analyses, their stock price will mostly depend on the price of silver and gold, with or without internal developments. During upsides with high commodity prices, almost every miner on the planet is doing very well, backed by higher margins and more cash flows, leading to more investments, higher dividends/buybacks, and more. But, when prices fall during a downcycle, we’ll see silver miners having a tougher time once again, with potential debt issues, lower production and even bankruptcies in some cases. AG, for example wasn’t doing that well a decade ago, when precious metal prices were down.

Their Q2 earnings report will be released on August 14, with estimates hinting at EPS of $0.05 and $282.72 million in revenue, a significant increase compared to last year thanks to the Los Gatos mine and better precious metals prices. I’ll be paying close attention especially to their free cash flow - which could increase significantly - as well as any news about the tax dispute and potential Gatos synergies.

As a note, during the Mining Forum Europe 2025 Conference on April 7, their CEO even stated that:

Obviously 2025 is going to be a record year for us. So, in 2025 we'll be producing somewhere in the neighborhood of 30 million Silver equivalent ounces... Our cash flows will be quite amazing actually.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Pan American Silver (PAAS), Hecla Mining (HL), MAG Silver (MAG), Endeavour Silver (EXK), Silvercorp Metals (SVM), Fortuna Mining (FSM) and Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF), we are looking at a relatively average valuation from almost every point of view. However, I think we should look into this again once all these companies release their Q2 data, as this can change the Price/CF ratios significantly, with all of them likely to record better numbers now thanks to record levels in silver and gold. Still, AG is likely to remain at a relatively average valuation compared to the rest in the short-term unless they jump significantly, so the earnings shouldn’t change the top that much, but rather offer a better idea about them on an absolute level.

Valuation

As for the valuation, I’ll adjust last year’s free cash flow to the 45.6% higher silver equivalent ounces production (vs. 2024, at midpoint), 30% higher silver/gold prices, lower costs minus the $78 million higher CAPEX, and we get to ~$75 million in FCF per year. This FCF is projected to then grow at a 5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their investments, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5. Assuming a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 6.5% - in line with the lower estimates in the market - and accounting for $182.6 million in net cash and 484.59 million shares outstanding, the resulting DCF model returns an enterprise value of ~$2.17 billion. After adding the net cash, this leads to an equity value of roughly $2.35 billion, giving an intrinsic value of about $4.85 per share, which is significantly above the current level.

Even a more optimistic $100 million in starting FCF wouldn’t change much, as that would result in a price of $6.34, still well below the current level.

Conclusions

In this market, I don’t see the potential in First Majestic Silver. I rate them a Hold now, as the company is healthy and the price will likely keep following the price of silver in the near term, but the potential seems very limited in the long run, as higher precious metal prices seem to be priced in, while the risks remain. There are simply better silver miners out there coming at better prices.

data.title: VUSB: A Top Choice For Your Emergency Fund
data.description:
A colleague recently asked me where I recommend keeping an emergency fund.

An easy answer might be a money market fund. There’s no share price to worry about, they offer same-day liquidity, and they're easy to access through most brokerage and retirement accounts. Right now, the better (non-promotional) funds are paying around 4.0–4.3%. That’s a decent return for a cash equivalent, but you can do better with minimal effort.

Certificates of deposit are another possibility, and I get the appeal: they’re FDIC insured, the rate is locked, and there’s no price volatility. Current yields are in the 4.25–4.5% range, which is on par with the Fed funds rate. The tradeoff is flexibility. Even at the short end, you’re committed to six months or more. I like them for teaching a new saver some discipline, but it's not ideal for a true “emergency” fund.

Instead, I prefer options with short duration that are liquid but also take advantage of T-Bills and the high-grade bond market. Going much beyond a year adds interest rate risk, which defeats the purpose if your goal is stability.

That’s where ultra-short bond ETFs (USBFs) come in. They sit between a money market and traditional bond funds, providing near-cash stability with a little more yield. Rather than parking cash in a MMF or locking it in a CD, I prefer USBFs for their combination of safety, liquidity, and yield.

In this corner of the market, there are a few different approaches, ranging from 100% Treasuries to blended portfolios to corporate‑only options. My top choice at the moment is the Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB), which I’ll come back to after sharing some data.

Ultra-Short Bond ETFs (USBFs)

USBFs hold securities from pure Treasury bills to blends of government, agency, and high-grade corporate debt. They have low price sensitivity to interest rate changes and carry minimal credit risk. They’re often used to manage cash in investment portfolios, but they’re also a great option for substantial savings like an emergency fund.

I break them into three categories:

Pure Treasury - These invest only in short-term U.S. Treasuries, making them the most secure option among USBFs. Their share prices rarely move, and credit risk is effectively zero. Current SEC yields are up to 4.25%. The main advantage over MMFs is the state and local tax exemption on interest (more on this shortly). Compared to CDs, they offer similar yields but without locking up your cash for months at a time.

Mixed Sector - Here we have a combination of Treasuries, agency bonds, and high‑grade corporate debt. They offer slightly more income for slightly more credit exposure, with current yields in the 4.45–4.55% range.

Corporate‑Only - This group targets investment‑grade corporate bonds and commercial paper, leaving out Treasuries and agencies. Credit risk is still extremely low at this end of the curve, but prices can shift a little during market stress. In exchange, they usually offer the highest yields among USBFs. Choice is limited in this category. The top option currently yields about 4.6%.

The Options

The list below includes ETFs with (1) short interest rate durations, preferably around or under a year, and (2) at least $2B in AUM for adequate liquidity. I also included the expense ratios, though the current 30-day SEC yields given are already net of fees. Here are the options sorted into the three categories:

Pure Treasury

** Best of the group: SGOV has the highest AUM and highest yield at 4.23%.

Mixed Sector

(PULS)

(VUSB)

** Best of the group: VUSB’s yield is a few basis points lower than PULS, but its slightly longer duration would hold on to today’s rates a bit longer.

Corporate‑Only
